flat.name: The Flat
flat.body: One room. Your table by the window. Rooftops, aerials, a sliver of basin water. The radiator clicks.

from_L01: Home. Or what I'm calling home.
The flat looks different every time.
from_L02: Home. Or what I'm calling home.
The notebook has more pages filled now.
Something to do today. Someone to see.

interactableDetails.description: A mug ring on the table. From before you.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: A perfect circle stained into the wood. Someone else sat here. Drank something. Left this behind without meaning to.

interactableDetails.description: Aerials on the rooftops. Thin lines against the sky.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The aerials catch nothing now. But they're still reaching. Still pointed at a sky that stopped answering.

interactableDetails.description: White paint on the floorboards, wearing through.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Someone painted these boards white. Underneath, the original wood is dark and warm. Two versions of this room, layered.

interactableDetails.description: Your notebook. Something written in the margins.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: You wrote it without thinking. The melody, in notation. You've been hearing it longer than you realised. Since before the coffee shop.

interactableDetails.description: A groove worn into the floorboard. Foot-shaped.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: A groove in the wood, where someone stood for hours. At a microphone, maybe. Or a music stand. This room was a studio once.

interactableDetails.description: A vibration in the wall. You can feel it now.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Your hand on the wall. The building hums. 47 hertz. The same as the canal, the lock gates, the studio. Your flat was part of it all along.

interactableDetails.description: A folded note in the door frame.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The walls talk if you tap them. Not words — impressions. The people here are worth knowing. Visit them. Come back. Walk slowly. Some things only appear in the rain. Some only at night. Good luck with the flat. The radiator has a rhythm. — M.

interactableDetails.description: Light angle on the table. Shifting.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The light moves across the table in a slow arc. It maps the building opposite through your window. A projection of someone else's home.

interactableDetails.description: A shadow on the wall. Too detailed.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The shadow on the wall is a perfect blueprint of the building opposite. Windows, drainpipes, aerials. A camera obscura you didn't build.

interactableDetails.description: A warm spot on the floor.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: One spot near the radiator holds heat long after it clicks off. Your bare feet find it every time. The building remembers where to be warm.

interactableDetails.description: A draft from a gap you can't see.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The draft is warm. Not from outside. From inside the wall. As if the building breathes and you've found its mouth. The air tastes of plaster and something older.

interactableDetails.description: A mug ring on the table. Not yours.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The ring is double-layered. Someone set a cup down, picked it up, set it down again. A habit. A person who hesitated before leaving.

interactableDetails.description: Voices through the wall.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: You press your ear to the wall. Not words. Rhythms. Greeting, argument, reconciliation. The same conversation the building has heard for decades.

interactableDetails.description: The door handle. Worn smooth.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The brass is worn to a mirror. Decades of hands, thousands of turns. The metal has been shaped by everyone who lived here. A handle made by use.

interactableDetails.description: The window latch. Older than it should be.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Victorian ironwork on a 1960s window frame. The latch was salvaged. Someone kept this one piece when everything else was torn down. It's the oldest thing in the flat, by a century.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain against the window. The basin blurs.
weatherEffects.clear: Light through the window shifts during your visit.

flat.bodySensation: The radiator clicks twice before heat arrives. The building takes its time.
flat.permanentPresence: The lamp on the table. Always on when you return.
flat.mythologicalImpression: Someone was here before you. The room holds the shape of their absence.

L01.name: The Canal Basin
L01.body: Diesel and wet stone. The water is flat. A chain clinks against a mooring ring. A heron, sometimes, perfectly still.

from_flat: Door. Street. London. Here we go.
The canal is outside. Always outside.
Basin still. Boats still. I'm still.
from_L02: The street narrows toward the basin.
A heron on the lock gate. Absolutely still.
from_L05: The pub smell fades. Diesel takes over.
From warm wood to cold stone.
from_L10: The lot behind me. The basin ahead.
Streetlight to water. Darker this way.

interactableDetails.description: A chain against a mooring ring.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The chain clinks in a rhythm. Not wind. Not water. Something underneath, keeping time.

interactableDetails.description: A name painted on the narrowboat hull.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The name has been painted over. Three times, at least. Each layer a different hand. The boat outlasts its owners.

interactableDetails.description: The lamp by the canal path.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The lamp hums. A low B-flat. The same note as the one in your head when you can't sleep.

interactableDetails.description: Something small on the towpath.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: A lens cap. 52mm. Scuffed on one side. No camera anywhere near it.

interactableDetails.description: A palette and cloth on the stone wall.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: A palette, paint still wet. A cloth folded on the stone beside it. Someone was here before you. Will be, again.

interactableDetails.description: The canal surface in rain. Reflections that don't match.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Raindrops on the canal. The reflections ripple. For a moment, the buildings in the water have different windows. A different Limehouse, underneath.

interactableDetails.description: The water itself. Not the surface. Below.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: You listen past the surface. Past the diesel. Past the lapping. There — a sustained note. Rising from the silt. The canal has been singing since before the city was named.

interactableDetails.description: Light on the canal surface.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The light breaks on the water into shapes that hold for a second. Long enough to frame. Too fast to capture.

interactableDetails.description: The bridge reflected in the basin.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The reflection shows two arches. You look up. One bridge. You look down. Two. The water remembers a bridge that was demolished.

interactableDetails.description: The towpath stone. Cold underfoot.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The stone is colder than the air. It pulls heat from your soles. Centuries of feet have polished it smooth. Your body fits into grooves worn by dockers.

interactableDetails.description: Mist off the water. On your skin.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The mist touches your face and the canal's full depth arrives through your skin. Every layer of silt, every sunken thing. Your skin reads the water like braille. The basin is deeper than any map says.

interactableDetails.description: A bench by the towpath. Worn smooth.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The bench is worn into two hollows. Side by side. The same two people, the same time, for years. You can see the shape of their friendship in the wood.

interactableDetails.description: A path worn into the grass beside the towpath.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The worn path tells a story. Morning dog-walkers go clockwise. Evening ones counter. They never meet. Two communities sharing a basin, perfectly out of phase. A city of near-misses.

interactableDetails.description: A mooring bollard. Dates stamped in iron.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: 1820. Stamped into the bollard. The iron was cast when this basin held cargo ships. The bollard has outlasted every boat it ever held.

interactableDetails.description: Bricks in the basin wall. Mismatched.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Three different bricks. Three different centuries. London stock, Staffordshire blue, modern engineering. The wall was repaired with whatever was at hand. Each brick a receipt for a different era's trouble.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain on the canal. A million tiny impacts. The heron doesn't move.
weatherEffects.clear: The water holds the sky. Every colour the sky gives away.

L01.bodySensation: The cold comes off the water within seconds of arriving.
L01.permanentPresence: The narrowboat is always moored here. It never moves.
L01.mythologicalImpression: The water has been the same temperature for one hundred years.

L02.name: The Coffee Shop
L02.body: Espresso and steam. The counter is scarred where something hot sat too long. One table by the window gets all the morning light.

from_flat: Coffee steam from the shop. A signal.
Espresso smell before the door opens.
Narrow Street is narrow. The name is honest.
from_L01: Espresso smell before the door opens.
The street narrows toward the shop.
from_L03: Stone to warmth. Incense to espresso.
The churchyard behind me. Coffee ahead.
From the dead to the living.

interactableDetails.description: A burn mark in the wood. Shaped like a quarter note.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The burn mark is deep. Someone set something hot down and left it. Years ago.

interactableDetails.description: The chalkboard menu. The handwriting changes character mid-word.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The handwriting shifts halfway through 'cappuccino.' Two people? Or one person who isn't always the same person.

interactableDetails.description: A cat on the doorstep. Watching nothing.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The cat watches a spot on the floor where nothing is. Or nothing you can see.

interactableDetails.description: Steam from the machine. Caught in light.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The steam catches the window light and becomes solid for an instant. A column of gold. The machine makes sculpture every thirty seconds and nobody frames it.

interactableDetails.description: The rectangle of light on the floor.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The window projects a perfect rectangle on the floorboards. Inside it, the dust is visible. Outside, nothing. A frame the sun built. It moves an inch every ten minutes.

interactableDetails.description: Your cup. The warmth through ceramic.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The heat moves through the ceramic in waves. Your palms map the cup's temperature like terrain. Hotter at the top. The handle stays cool. Your body reads it all.

interactableDetails.description: A soft spot in the floor by the counter.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The floor dips where hundreds of people have stood to order. Your feet find the groove without looking. The building has shaped itself around a daily ritual.

interactableDetails.description: A table in the corner. Always the same cups.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Two flat whites, one black. Same table, same time. They don't speak much. They don't need to. The order says everything — who's fragile today, who slept badly.

interactableDetails.description: The tip jar. Notes folded inside.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Someone folds their notes into cranes before dropping them in. Every morning. A tiny gift inside the gift.

interactableDetails.description: The counter wood. Reclaimed from somewhere.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Ship's timber. You can see the bolt holes, filled but visible. This counter was a deck once. It's been sold, salvaged, and sold again. The wood has had more owners than the building.

interactableDetails.description: The espresso machine. Not what it seems.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: A 1970s Faema. Rebuilt twice, new gaskets, original boiler. Worth more than the lease. Someone brought this from Naples. The serial number is filed off — it has a history it's hiding.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain streaks on the window. The interior feels warmer by contrast.
weatherEffects.clear: Sunlight through the window makes a rectangle on the floor that moves during your visit.

L02.bodySensation: The coffee shop is warm in a way that marks how cold it was outside.
L02.permanentPresence: The table by the window. Empty. It waits.
L02.mythologicalImpression: Everything here has a history it is not showing. The counter. The cups. The light.

L03.name: St Anne's Churchyard
L03.body: The church is too large for this street. Stone stained in deliberate-looking patterns. Grass between graves always damp.

from_L02: The church rises. Too tall for here.
Hawksmoor. Even the name is heavy.
from_L04: The studio noise fades. The quiet isn't.
from_L05: Pub warmth gone. Stone cold here.
The churchyard at this hour. Brave.
Hawksmoor's shadow. Always longer than expected.

interactableDetails.description: The church's shadow on the ground.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Count the sides of the shadow. Count the sides of the church. The numbers don't match.

interactableDetails.description: Grass between the graves. Always damp.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The grass hasn't dried in weeks. It hasn't rained in five days. The ground here holds onto things.

interactableDetails.description: Stains on the church stone. Too deliberate for weather.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The stains run in intervals. Like notation on a staff. Someone wrote music on a church in a language no one remembers.

interactableDetails.description: A sticker on the back of a gravestone.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: A library archive sticker. Catalogue number: LMA/4521/B/03/012. Someone was counting these.

interactableDetails.description: An inscription on a gravestone. Visible only when wet.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The rain fills the letters carved into stone. A name. A date. And below: 'Still listening.' The rain stops. The letters vanish.

interactableDetails.description: The church tower. Listening back.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: You stand still. The tower is not silent. It resonates. A low B-flat, sustained, as if the stone itself is remembering a congregation that sang here centuries ago.

interactableDetails.description: Light through the church windows. A geometry on the ground.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The window casts a shape on the gravel that doesn't match the glass. Extra lines. A pattern the architect hid in the glazing, visible only at this angle, this hour.

interactableDetails.description: The spire's shadow. Longer than it should be.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: You measure the shadow against the church. It's twelve feet too long. You check your own shadow — correct. Only the church casts more darkness than its height allows. Hawksmoor knew.

interactableDetails.description: The stone wall. Damp to the touch.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Your hand on the church wall. The stone is wet, but not from rain. It sweats. Cold and steady, like a body with a fever breaking. The building is alive in some way stone shouldn't be.

interactableDetails.description: The grass underfoot. Pulling.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Your feet sink slightly with each step. Not mud. The ground here is soft in a way that has nothing to do with water. It yields. It wants you closer. The churchyard remembers how to receive.

interactableDetails.description: Fresh flowers on a grave. No name visible.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Roses. Fresh this morning. The grave has no legible name. Someone still visits. Every week, the same flowers. A relationship outlasting the stone that was meant to preserve it.

interactableDetails.description: A bench near the path. Carved initials.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Initials carved over decades. Some deep, some shallow. Some crossed out. You trace the oldest: 1923. Two letters intertwined. Someone sat here and declared something. The churchyard heard.

interactableDetails.description: The churchyard gate. Hand-forged hinges.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Hand-forged hinges. No two the same. The smith's hammer marks are still visible. These were made for this gate, this church, this exact weight of iron. Bespoke. Three hundred years old.

interactableDetails.description: A stone near the base. Different from the others.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Portland stone at the base, but this one block is older. Roman. Reused. You can see the chisel marks — two styles, two millennia apart. Someone built Hawksmoor's church on top of something that was already here.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain on stone. The church darkens. The graves darken more.
weatherEffects.clear: The shadow of the spire reaches further than the building is tall.

L03.bodySensation: The grass is damp underfoot even when it has not rained in days.
L03.permanentPresence: Fresh flowers on the nearest grave. Every time.
L03.mythologicalImpression: Hawksmoor built this church taller than it needed to be. He had reasons.

L04.name: The Warehouse Studio
L04.body: Concrete and cable. Frequency printouts pinned over each other like geological strata on one wall. The space hums.

from_L03: Stone to concrete. Older to newer.
The studio door. Always slightly open.
from_L05: Up the street. The hum gets louder.
From old wood to new concrete.

interactableDetails.description: Tape on the studio door. A handwritten rule.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Masking tape on the door frame. Four words in marker: NO PHONES NO CLOCKS. Not a request. A principle. Someone decided time works differently in here.

interactableDetails.description: Frequency printouts layered on the wall.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: One printout underneath the others. A sustained note at 47Hz. Recorded at the canal. Nothing in the canal makes that sound.

interactableDetails.description: The space itself. It hums.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The room resonates at a frequency that matches your chest. You can feel it in your sternum. This building remembers sound.

interactableDetails.description: A shaft of light from the high window. Dust suspended.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The dust doesn't settle. It hangs in the light like a developing photograph. Each particle a tiny mirror.

interactableDetails.description: The dark monitor. Your reflection, distorted.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The monitor is off but its surface holds your reflection. Curved, stretched. Behind you, the room looks different in the black glass. Deeper. Older. A warehouse before it was a studio.

interactableDetails.description: The concrete floor. A different kind of cold.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The concrete holds the cold of last night. Your feet feel the building's thermal memory — warm where equipment sat, cold where nothing has been for months. A heat map of use.

interactableDetails.description: The air. Heavier in here.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The air is denser inside the studio. Your ears adjust. The building is sealed tighter than it looks. Designed to hold sound in. Or keep something out.

interactableDetails.description: Coffee cups on a shelf. A collection.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Seven cups. Different shops, different hands. Each one a late-night session, a visitor, a collaboration. The cups are a guest book nobody meant to keep.

interactableDetails.description: Names written on tape on the mixing desk.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Masking tape labels, layered. Names of everyone who recorded here. Some crossed out. Some written over. A history of creative partnerships made and broken on a strip of tape.

interactableDetails.description: A cable coil. The connector is old.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: DIN connector. 1960s. The cable is worth more than the equipment it's plugged into. Someone has been hoarding studio gear since before this building was converted. Collector's instinct.

interactableDetails.description: A printout under the others. Older paper.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The paper is yellowed. Dot-matrix print. 1987. A frequency analysis of this exact room, done decades before the studio existed. Someone knew what this space was before anyone recorded here.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain on the warehouse roof. Amplified. The concrete holds every drop.
weatherEffects.clear: Dust in a shaft of light from the high window. The particles don't settle.

L04.bodySensation: The concrete holds last night's cold. Your feet find the warm patches near equipment.
L04.permanentPresence: The printouts on the wall. More of them each time.
L04.mythologicalImpression: The room was already recording before anyone brought equipment in.

L05.name: The Grapes
L05.body: Low ceiling, wood almost black. The Thames visible through a back window, moving. Floorboards give. Everything touched ten thousand times.

from_L01: The pub. Oldest thing in Limehouse.
Wood and river. The Grapes endures.
Low door. Duck your head. Enter.
from_L03: From the churchyard to the pub. Logical.
The pub is warmer. Always warmer.
from_L04: Concrete to wood. The old world.
The Grapes has outlasted everything around it.
From studio to pub. The usual route.
from_L06: Ink to beer. A good evening.
The pub after the parlour. Tradition.

interactableDetails.description: The floorboards. They give under your weight.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The wood gives like a living thing. Four hundred years of feet have taught these boards to yield.

interactableDetails.description: The back window. The Thames, moving.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The river through glass. It never stops. The pub was built to watch this. Someone has been watching for centuries.

interactableDetails.description: A door behind the bar. The cellar.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Sound rises from the cellar. Not barrels settling. A resonance. As if the foundation stones are singing to each other.

interactableDetails.description: The cellar door. Something behind it.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: You press your ear to the cellar door. Music. Faint, layered, decades old. A woman's voice singing in a dialect you almost understand. The tapes. They're still playing.

interactableDetails.description: Light off the Thames, flickering on the ceiling.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: River light dances on the ceiling. The pub becomes a camera obscura. The Thames projects itself inside, upside down, shimmering. Four hundred years of this. The oldest moving picture in London.

interactableDetails.description: Your glass. The room bends inside it.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Through the curved glass, the pub warps. The timber bends. The window stretches. And for a moment you see the room as it was — lower ceiling, rushlight, faces you don't recognise. The glass remembers an older pub.

interactableDetails.description: The bar top. Warm to the touch.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The wood is warm. Not from the room. From inside. Centuries of hands have given this bar their heat. It radiates back. You lean on it and feel held.

interactableDetails.description: The floor. Tilted toward the river.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The floor tilts. Two degrees, toward the Thames. Your body adjusts without thinking. The building has been leaning riverward for centuries.

interactableDetails.description: A couple in the corner. Not speaking.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: They sit in silence. Comfortable silence. She turns her glass. He watches the river. Twenty years of marriage in the way they occupy space without needing to fill it.

interactableDetails.description: Scratches on a table. Tally marks.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Tally marks scratched into the table. Hundreds. Not drinks. Visits. Someone counted every time they came here. The marks stop at 1,247. You wonder what made them stop counting.

interactableDetails.description: The ceiling beam. Darker than the rest.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The sea is in the ceiling. Salt-cured oak. The beam was a keel once — you can see where the ribs joined.

interactableDetails.description: The brass tap. Green around the base.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The patina tells a story. Green at the base where beer splashed for decades. Bright where hands gripped. The tap has been here since 1880. It's poured every ale London has brewed since.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain on the old windows. The Thames rises. The pub feels like a ship.
weatherEffects.clear: Light off the Thames flickers through the back window.

L05.bodySensation: The floorboards give under your weight. Four centuries of feet have taught them to yield.
L05.permanentPresence: The back window. The Thames through it, moving. Always.
L05.mythologicalImpression: The pub was here before the street. The street before the city. The city before the name.

L06.name: The Tattoo Parlour
L06.body: Antiseptic and ink. Buzzing from behind the curtain. Flash art on walls: not anchors or skulls. Doors. Maps. Things that go somewhere.

from_L05: The arch under the railway. Buzzing inside.
Doors on the walls. Maps of nowhere.

interactableDetails.description: Flash art on the wall. Doors. All of them.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Every piece of flash art is a door. Not decorative. Specific. Doors you could walk through if they existed. Some look familiar.

interactableDetails.description: The buzzing from behind the curtain.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The needle buzzes in C-sharp. Always C-sharp. The artist doesn't notice. But the note carries through the wall like a signal.

interactableDetails.description: Neon light on the far wall. A red wash.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The neon turns the back wall into a darkroom. Everything red, shadow-heavy. The flash art looks different in this light. The doors on the wall look open.

interactableDetails.description: A mirror on the wall. Angled wrong.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The mirror doesn't show the room. It shows the street through the window. Angled to catch the railway arch. A surveillance frame. Or a portrait of the view the artist prefers.

interactableDetails.description: The tattoo chair. Vinyl warm from the last client.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The vinyl is warm. Someone was here ten minutes ago. The chair holds their shape, their heat. You sit in the ghost of a stranger's decision to be marked.

interactableDetails.description: The ink smell. Sharper near the curtain.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Antiseptic and iron. The ink smells like blood's cousin. Your body recognises something old in the scent. Marking skin is ancient. The parlour knows it.

interactableDetails.description: Three chairs in the waiting area. Positioned.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The chairs face each other, not the door. People who wait here end up talking. Strangers bonding over nerves. The artist arranged this on purpose.

interactableDetails.description: Polaroids on the wall. Finished work.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Hundreds of polaroids. Every piece of finished work. Some clients are in three, four, five photos. Coming back. The wall is a map of loyalty. Of trust built one needle-session at a time.

interactableDetails.description: Flash art in the corner. The paper is yellowed.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: This sheet is hand-drawn. 1950s, at least. Sailor flash from Limehouse's maritime years. Worth something to a collector. The artist keeps it on the wall like a family photograph.

interactableDetails.description: The tattoo machine on the tray. Not modern.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: A coil machine. Hand-wound. The frame is stamped with a name you can't read and a date: 1971. In an age of rotaries, the artist still uses this. Some tools earn the right to stay.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain on the railway arch above. The buzz inside competes.
weatherEffects.clear: Neon reflects on the wet pavement outside. Even when it hasn't rained.

L06.bodySensation: Antiseptic reaches the back of your throat before the door is fully open.
L06.permanentPresence: The flash art doors on the wall. Still there. Still open.
L06.mythologicalImpression: The doors on the walls look like they go somewhere. They might.

L07.name: The Lock Gates
L07.body: Iron, massive, dripping. The mechanism is Victorian and works. Water pushes against the gates like breathing.

from_L06: From ink to iron. Both hold.
The lock gates. Heavy even to look at.
from_L08: Down from the platform. River level.
The gates hold the canal. Just.
Iron against water. An old argument.

interactableDetails.description: The lock gate mechanism. Victorian ironwork.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The mechanism turns. Slowly. But there's no one operating it. No water pressure to explain the movement. It just turns.

interactableDetails.description: The iron gate. Cold even in summer.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: You touch the iron. It's cold. Not metal-cold. Actively cold. As if something on the other side is drawing heat through.

interactableDetails.description: Scratches in the iron.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Small marks scratched into the iron. Not graffiti — too deliberate. Arrows, distances, a symbol you don't recognise. Someone was routing something through here.

interactableDetails.description: The lock mechanism in rain. A different sound.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Rain on iron. The lock gates vibrate at a frequency you almost recognise. 47 hertz. The same as the canal recording. The gates remember.

interactableDetails.description: The water between the gates. A perfect mirror.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Between the gates, the water is completely still. A perfect mirror. The sky below is sharper than the sky above. As if the real world is the reflection.

interactableDetails.description: Rust patterns on the gate. Like a map.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The rust has made a map. Rivers of orange and brown tracing the iron's grain. A topography of decay that looks like the waterways on an old Ordnance Survey. The gate is drawing its own surroundings.

interactableDetails.description: The gate. Your hand on the iron.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Your palm on the gate. The iron vibrates — not from the mechanism. From the water pressing against the other side. You can feel the canal's weight through your hand. The river wants through.

interactableDetails.description: Spray from the lock. On your face.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The spray is not cold. It should be. Canal water in winter. But it touches your face warm. As if the water passed through something heated on its way up. The lock gates are sweating.

interactableDetails.description: A smooth patch on the stone. Someone sits here.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The stone is worn smooth in a seated shape. The same fisherman, same spot, every Sunday. Nobody catches anything here. He comes for the company of the water.

interactableDetails.description: A padlock on the railing. Initials scratched.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: One padlock among none. Not a bridge tradition — someone started one here, alone. The initials are scratched deep. J+M. No other locks joined it. A declaration that stayed singular.

interactableDetails.description: A bolt in the mechanism. Newer than the rest.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Stainless steel among cast iron. One bolt replaced, recently. Everything else is original. Victorian engineers built this to last. Only one piece failed in two hundred years.

interactableDetails.description: A mark stamped into the counterweight.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: A foundry mark. Coalbrookdale. The same ironworks that built the first iron bridge. This counterweight was cast in 1812 and shipped down the canal it would spend its life operating. It arrived at its own workplace.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain on iron. Every surface drips. The canal rises.
weatherEffects.clear: Mist off the water. The gates stand in it like monuments.

L07.bodySensation: The spray from the lock reaches you before you expect it. Canal water, warm.
L07.permanentPresence: The gates hold. The water presses against them. The argument continues.
L07.mythologicalImpression: The gates have been holding back the same water since before London had a name.

L08.name: The DLR Platform
L08.body: Automated announcement repeating. Wind through the platform, channelled and sharp. Pigeons own the rafters. Rooftops and cranes from here.

from_L07: Steps up. The platform. Higher ground.
The DLR sees everything. Rooftops. Cranes.
from_G09: Up the ramp. Leaving the river behind.
The DLR glides in. No driver.
from_L09: The market noise fades. Platform quiet.
Up the steps. London spreads out.
The train comes when it comes.

interactableDetails.description: The departure board. Something wrong with the display.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Next train: The Meridian. 3 min. That station doesn't exist. You blink. The board reads Canary Wharf again.

interactableDetails.description: Wind through the platform. Channelled.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The wind plays the platform like an instrument. The pitch changes when a train approaches. But this time there's no train.

interactableDetails.description: Cranes on the skyline. A composition.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The cranes frame Canary Wharf like a photograph someone arranged. Steel and glass and sky. From up here, London looks like it's still being assembled. An unfinished exposure.

interactableDetails.description: Rain streaks on the shelter glass.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The rain traces paths down the glass that the next drop follows. The same routes, reinforced. The shelter has developed its own drainage pattern. A long-exposure written in water.

interactableDetails.description: The wind. Sharper up here.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The wind funnels through the platform and finds every gap in your clothing. Your body tightens. Up here, nothing between you and the skyline. The city can see you and you can see it. An exchange.

interactableDetails.description: The platform underfoot. Vibrating.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: A train three stations away. You feel it in your feet before the board updates. The platform transmits the whole network through its concrete. Your body is a receiver.

interactableDetails.description: Worn patches on the platform surface.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The concrete is lighter in three spots. Where the doors stop. Every commuter walks to the same position. Thousands of people, the same three marks. A city of habits pretending to be choices.

interactableDetails.description: People on the bench. Spaced exactly.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Three strangers, equidistant. No one agreed. No one measured. But the gap between each is identical. London's unspoken rules of proximity, enforced by instinct.

interactableDetails.description: A tile on the wall. Different from the others.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: One tile is older. Deeper glaze, hand-cut edge. Salvaged from the station this one replaced. The builders kept one piece of the original. A receipt hidden in the renovation.

interactableDetails.description: The station sign. Paint layers visible at the edge.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Five colours at the edge of the sign. Five rebrands. Docklands, DLR, TfL. Each layer a different era's idea of what this place was for. The station has been sold to itself five times.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain on the platform canopy. Sheets of it past the edge.
weatherEffects.clear: Cranes on the skyline. The city builds itself in every direction.

L08.bodySensation: The wind at platform level finds every gap in your clothing. The city can see you.
L08.permanentPresence: The departure board cycles. The train comes and goes without a driver.
L08.mythologicalImpression: From up here, London looks unfinished. It has always looked unfinished.

L09.name: The Night Market
L09.body: Generators and LED strings. Charcoal and chili smoke. Voices competing. The pavement vibrates from bass. Everything temporary, gone by morning.

from_L08: Down from the platform. Into noise.
Generators and bass. Limehouse at night.
from_B09: Back through the tunnel. Limehouse smells different now.
from_L10: From silence to everything. The market.
The lot was empty. This is full.

interactableDetails.description: A generator. Humming under everything.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The generator hums at the same frequency as the studio. And the cellar. And the canal chain. The whole neighbourhood is tuned.

interactableDetails.description: LED strings overhead. Swaying.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The lights sway in a pattern. Not wind. Not passing trucks. A rhythm. As if someone is conducting them from somewhere you can't see.

interactableDetails.description: Charcoal smoke in LED light.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The smoke from the grills catches the LED strings and becomes solid for an instant. Columns of light where there was nothing.

interactableDetails.description: A puddle. Neon reflected.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The puddle holds every light source at once. LEDs, phone screens, the glow from a food stall. A composite portrait of the market, compressed into three inches of rainwater.

interactableDetails.description: Heat from the food stalls. Waves of it.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: You walk through pockets of heat. Each stall has its own climate. Charcoal, gas, wood fire. Your skin reads the market's geography in temperature. A map of flame.

interactableDetails.description: The bass. You feel it in your chest.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The bass speaker is somewhere to your left. You don't hear it. You feel it. Your ribcage becomes a resonating chamber. The market uses your body as an instrument.

interactableDetails.description: The queue at the most popular stall.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The queue moves in pulses. Three orders, pause, three more. The cook works in sets. The queue has learned the rhythm without knowing it. They step forward in unison.

interactableDetails.description: Vendors across the aisle. A glance between them.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Two vendors nod across the crowd. A micro-conversation. Price check? Warning? Friendship? The market has its own language, spoken in glances. You're learning to read it.

interactableDetails.description: The stall frame. Welded and rewelded.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The frame has been welded four times. Each joint a different style. Four different stalls, four different businesses. The frame outlasts what it holds. Someone keeps buying the same bones.

interactableDetails.description: Spice jars on a stall. Labels handwritten.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The jars are old. Kilner, pre-war. The spices inside are fresh but the glass has been in continuous use since someone's grandmother. Each jar a family supply chain, disguised as a market stall.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain and LED light. Everything reflects. The market shines.
weatherEffects.clear: Smoke rises into nothing. The market is its own weather.

L09.bodySensation: Heat comes in waves off the food stalls. Your face reads the geography in temperature.
L09.permanentPresence: The LED strings sway in a rhythm. Not wind.
L09.mythologicalImpression: Everything here will be gone by morning. The night market knows this. It doesn't mind.

L10.name: The Empty Lot
L10.body: Silence. Weeds through cracked concrete. Adjacent walls show where a building used to be. A scar in the brick. The sky bigger here.

from_L09: Empty space. London forgot this place.
Weeds and silence. A gap in the city.
from_B09: The tunnel behind me. Limehouse ahead.
from_L01: Past the basin. Into the gap.
The lot. Nothing here. Everything here.

interactableDetails.description: The scar in the brick where a building was.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The outline of rooms. A fireplace. A staircase. All gone. But the wall remembers the shape of someone else's life.

interactableDetails.description: Weeds through cracked concrete.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The weeds grow in a line. Following something underground. A pipe, maybe. Or a foundation. Or a path that used to be here.

interactableDetails.description: The silence itself. The sky bigger here.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Stand still. Listen. There's a note here. Not sound — the absence of it. A silence that has shape. Like a room that was never built.

interactableDetails.description: A stake in the concrete. Orange paint, faded.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: A survey stake, driven into a crack in the concrete. Orange paint faded to pink. A number stamped on the cap: OS TQ 36820 80312. Someone measured this exact spot.

interactableDetails.description: The sky through the gap. Framed by walls.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The adjacent walls frame the sky like a viewfinder. A rectangle of cloud, perfectly composed. The demolished building left a camera behind.

interactableDetails.description: Shadows on the exposed wall. Room-shaped.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: At this hour, the shadows cast by the remaining walls create rooms on the ground. Ghost architecture. You can see where the kitchen was, the bedroom, the hallway. A floor plan drawn in darkness.

interactableDetails.description: The concrete. Warmer than it should be.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The cracked concrete radiates heat even in the evening. It holds the day's sun like a memory. Your feet find the warm patches. The lot's floor is a thermal diary of the hours you missed.

interactableDetails.description: The wind in the open space. It finds you.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The wind moves differently here. Without a building to break it, it arrives unfiltered. Your whole body feels the direction it came from. East. Always east. Off the river, through the gap. The lot is a wind tunnel built by absence.

interactableDetails.description: Scuff marks in the dirt. Not human.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Fox tracks. A family. They've divided the lot into territories — you can see the paths they take, the corners they avoid. A community in a space humans abandoned. The foxes hold meetings here.

interactableDetails.description: A makeshift shelter against the wall. Small.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Cardboard, a milk crate, a blanket. A kids' den. Built with care. Sweet wrappers inside, arranged like furniture. Someone small made a home in a place adults see as empty. The lot has tenants.

interactableDetails.description: A doorstep. Still here. The door is not.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: A granite doorstep, worn into a dip by decades of shoes. The building is gone but the threshold remains. Someone crossed this stone every morning for forty years. The step is still waiting.

interactableDetails.description: A fragment of tile in the rubble.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Encaustic tile. Victorian. A geometric pattern in red and cream. One piece of a hallway floor. Worth something to a reclamation yard. Worth more here, where it still belongs to the house that died.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain falls where a roof used to be. The outline is visible in the dry patches.
weatherEffects.clear: The sky is bigger here. Where the building was, nothing blocks it.

L10.bodySensation: The sky is larger here. Your body feels the absence of a roof.
L10.permanentPresence: The survey stake is still in the crack. The lot is still waiting.
L10.mythologicalImpression: A building stood here. You can still feel where the walls were.

G01.name: The Clockmaker's Shop
G01.body: Oil and brass. Forty clocks ticking not quite in time. The floor creaks left of the door. Dust in one high window's light.

from_G03: From bench to brass. The ticking begins.
Oil and old wood. The clocks know I'm here.
Each clock on its own time.
from_G07: From paper to mechanism. Both keep things.
The clockmaker's door. Always slightly ajar.
Forty ticking things. Almost in time.

interactableDetails.description: One clock on the back wall. Not moving.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Every clock in the shop is ticking. Every clock except this one. The hands are stopped at 4:17. They have been stopped at 4:17 for longer than the clockmaker will say.

interactableDetails.description: A small brass key on the shelf.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The key has no tag. No case number. The metal is worn to a shine in the grip. It has been held often. Carried. Put back. It fits nothing obvious in the shop.

interactableDetails.description: The number seven case. Something in it.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The case hums when you stand close. Not the clock inside — the wood itself. A frequency the clock didn't cause. The case is amplifying something the room contains.

interactableDetails.description: A drawer slightly open. Old clock faces inside.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Clock faces in the drawer. Dozens. Each one replaced at some point. The oldest have hand-painted numerals, the paint faded to whisper. The clockmaker keeps them. He doesn't throw away what once told time.

interactableDetails.description: The floor. Creaks left of the door.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: You step on the loose board. The creak is lower than it should be — too resonant for a single board. Something below amplifies it. The clockmaker doesn't react. He's heard it ten thousand times.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain on the window. The clocks speed up slightly. The clockmaker notices.
weatherEffects.clear: Morning light through the high window. Dust in columns. The mechanisms flash.

G01.bodySensation: The sound of forty clocks fills your chest. Not quite together. Almost.
G01.permanentPresence: The stopped clock. Always stopped. Always 4:17.
G01.mythologicalImpression: The shop has been measuring time for two hundred years. It keeps better time than any single clock inside it.

G02.name: The Observatory Terrace
G02.body: Wind always. The city below like something spilled. Cold stone underfoot. The brass meridian line warm from a day of hands.

from_G08: The crest. The dome ahead. The instruments.
The meridian line. The whole city organised around it.

interactableDetails.description: The brass meridian line in the ground.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The meridian line bisects the courtyard. Tourists stand on it and grin. You stand on it and feel the city reorganise itself around the line you're standing on. East and west. The line is the reason.

interactableDetails.description: The meridian. Something slightly wrong.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: You crouch over the meridian line. The brass is worn, true. But the wear pattern doesn't center on the line. It's offset by what the Observatory Keeper would call eleven seconds of arc. You stand up. The city looks the same.

interactableDetails.description: London spread below. Everything at once.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The city from up here. Glass towers and church spires and council estates and cranes. All of it together, all at once, none of it dominant. The Observatory was built to see all of this and measure it. The measurement hasn't stopped.

interactableDetails.description: The transit room door. A sound inside.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Your ear near the transit room door. 440 hertz. Concert A. A pure sustained tone from inside a room full of instruments that measure angles and time. Nothing in there should produce this. Nothing should sustain it.

interactableDetails.description: The stone underfoot. Cold even in sun.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The terrace stone is cold through your shoes even on a warm afternoon. The hill holds something that the sun can't warm. Not damp — something older than damp. The stone was here before the observatory. It remembers a different purpose.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain on the dome. The city below disappears into cloud. You're in the cloud.
weatherEffects.clear: The city spread below in clear light. Every building named by its shadow.

G02.bodySensation: Wind at this elevation finds every gap. The city hums below you.
G02.permanentPresence: The meridian line. Tourists standing on it. Grinning.
G02.mythologicalImpression: Greenwich tells the whole planet what time it is. The planet agreed. Nobody asked why.

G03.name: The Bench by St Alfege
G03.body: Worn wood, one armrest smooth from decades of the same elbow. The church wall holds heat after the sun moves.

from_G01: From the ticking to the quiet.
Stone and worn wood. Both hold.
from_G05: From the market into the churchyard. The noise drops.

interactableDetails.description: The left armrest. Smooth from one elbow.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The left armrest is worn to a gleam. Decades of the same person. The same arm. The same hours. The wood has taken the shape of someone's waiting. You can feel the indentation.

interactableDetails.description: The church wall. Three windows.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Three windows. But there's a shadow where a fourth should be — the outline of a blocked arch, older than the current wall. The church had four windows. Now it has three. Nobody thought to ask where the fourth went.

interactableDetails.description: The church wall. Warm to the touch.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Your hand on the church wall. The sun moved away from this face an hour ago. The stone is still warm. Not residual heat — something ongoing. The wall generates warmth it didn't receive.

interactableDetails.description: Something tucked under the bench slat.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: A page folded tight. Torn from a journal. The handwriting controlled but the content not: 'I erased them because the alternative was worse. A single node carrying every connection in London. I broke the web to save it. I broke it.'

interactableDetails.description: The bench wood. Worn smooth in the same place by the same person.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The wood is worn to a different texture on the left side. Decades of the same weight. The same arm resting in the same place. Someone has been coming here for a very long time.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain on the bench. The old man stays.
weatherEffects.clear: Late sun on the church wall. Gold stone.

G03.bodySensation: The bench wood is warm from the afternoon. Smooth where the same hands always rest.
G03.permanentPresence: The old man, at dusk. Right shoe untied. Always.
G03.mythologicalImpression: Someone has been sitting on this bench for much longer than the bench has existed.

G04.name: Greenwich Foot Tunnel
G04.body: Tiles, cream and green, curving down. Footsteps echo twice. The air is cooler than the street. Damp. Iron taste.

from_G09: The tunnel entrance. Down into the river crossing.
Cream and green tiles. The echo starts.
Under the Thames. The river above my head.

interactableDetails.description: The tiles. Cream and green. Curving.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Every tile the same — cream body, green border. They've been replaced piecemeal. You can see the joins: newer tiles slightly brighter, the grout a different shade. The tunnel has been maintained continuously for a century. Tile by tile.

interactableDetails.description: The midpoint. Something slows your step.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: You stop walking. Not because you chose to. Your legs simply slow at this exact point — the deepest part, directly under the river's centre. The clay around you presses. Not physically. Emotionally. Something in the ground here doesn't want you to pass.

interactableDetails.description: Your footsteps. They echo twice.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Your footstep arrives, then its echo, then a second echo from below. Below, not from the walls. The tunnel is a single tube. There's nothing below to reflect from. You step again. Three footsteps from one foot.

interactableDetails.description: The air. Iron in it.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The tunnel air has a metallic taste. Not rust — the pipes are maintained. Something older. The Thames above is tidal, salt and silt and time. You're breathing the compressed history of what's above you.

interactableDetails.description: The light strips overhead. Identical. Uniform. Wrong.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Fluorescent strips, evenly spaced. They should make the tunnel feel modern. They make it feel older. As if the artificial light is maintaining something that the natural world has stopped maintaining.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain falls above you. Here: still. The tunnel holds its quiet.
weatherEffects.clear: The tunnel doesn't know about weather. It's always this.

G04.bodySensation: Your footsteps return to you from below. The air tastes of iron and old river.
G04.permanentPresence: The echo that comes from the wrong direction.
G04.mythologicalImpression: The tunnel passes through the place the river was before the city drained it. The clay remembers.

G05.name: The Covered Market
G05.body: Canvas and chatter. Coffee from three directions. The stone floor uneven where carts wore it. Someone's radio under the stalls.

from_G06: From symmetry to chaos. The market.
Canvas and chatter. The market holds.
from_G03: From the churchyard into the noise.
from_G07: From quiet paper to loud things.
The bookshop to the market. Both have things.
Objects and coffee and the uneven floor.

interactableDetails.description: The stone floor. Grooves worn by cart wheels.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The grooves in the stone run parallel, cart-width apart. Centuries of market deliveries, the same route, the same wheels, until the stone gave way. The floor records every Tuesday market since it was laid.

interactableDetails.description: Coffee steam from three different stalls.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Three coffee stalls within twenty metres. Each makes the same drink differently. You can tell without tasting: one overextracted, one too milky, one right. The one that's right has a queue. People know without knowing.

interactableDetails.description: Someone's laptop screen in the corner.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: A laptop in the far corner, screen angled away. You catch a glimpse: not work, not a film. A map. London, annotated with hundreds of small marks — a pattern you can't read from this distance. The owner looks up. You look away.

interactableDetails.description: A radio under the stalls. Out of time.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The radio plays something that isn't a current station. The frequency is off — you're hearing the ghost of a signal that hasn't been broadcast for decades. The stall holder is singing along. He doesn't notice anything wrong.

interactableDetails.description: Steam rising from a coffee cup on the counter. The market awake.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Three stalls open. Two people talking to nobody in particular. A dog asleep under a trestle table. The covered market in the morning has the specific warmth of a place that has been doing the same thing for a very long time.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain on the canvas awnings. The market continues. It always continues.
weatherEffects.clear: Light through the awning gaps. The stone floor in patches of sun.

G05.bodySensation: The uneven floor shifts your weight. Coffee steam and voices from all angles.
G05.permanentPresence: The data scientist's corner table. The screen angled away.
G05.mythologicalImpression: Markets have happened on this ground since before the canvas. The ground expects it.

G06.name: The Naval College Courtyard
G06.body: Symmetry so complete it feels like pressure. White stone, scrubbed. Your footsteps sound important here. Gulls above.

from_G10: From the warmth to the white stone.
Gulls and architecture. The river behind.
from_G05: From the market's noise. This is engineered quiet.
The Naval College. The city steps back here.
White stone and planned sight lines.

interactableDetails.description: The Painted Hall entrance. The door is open.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The hall inside is overwhelmingly painted. Every surface occupied. Allegorical figures, naval victories, the heavens arranged to flatter earthly power. The ceiling is 40,000 square feet. Somewhere in the north-east corner, something doesn't add up.

interactableDetails.description: The north-east corner of the painted ceiling.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Behind Neptune's trident in the north-east corner: a brushstroke in a colour that doesn't exist in any other part of the painting. A pigment not manufactured until 1923. The 1707 ceiling contains a 1923 correction. The restorers called it contamination. It's too precise to be contamination.

interactableDetails.description: The symmetry. Columns mirrored exactly.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The courtyard is designed to be photographed. Every element has a counterpart. The symmetry is so complete it begins to feel like pressure — like standing inside a mirror that reflects itself. You walk to the centre. Both sides are identical. You are the only asymmetry.

interactableDetails.description: The cobbles. A pattern in the layout.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The cobbles are laid in a specific pattern — not random, not just decorative. A geometry that repeats at larger and larger scales. A fractal planned in stone. The pattern is too complex for coincidence and too old for computer assistance. Someone worked this out by hand.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain on the white stone. The symmetry sharpens.
weatherEffects.clear: Sunlight on the courtyard. The shadow lines double the architecture.

G06.bodySensation: The symmetry presses on both sides equally. Your footsteps sound formal.
G06.permanentPresence: The gulls overhead. The river beyond the arch.
G06.mythologicalImpression: This courtyard was built to make visitors feel small and the institution large. It still works.

G07.name: The Bookshop
G07.body: Paper and glue and the warmth of a space that hasn't been cold in decades. Shelves leaning. The owner's chair permanently dented.

from_G01: From the ticking to the silence of paper.
from_G05: From the market's chatter into this.
Paper and quiet. The market recedes.
The bookshop knows something the market doesn't.
from_G08: Down from the hill. Into the books.
The park to the shelves. Both full.
The bookshop. End of the hill walk.

interactableDetails.description: A journal on the shelf. 1983. Creased spine.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Journal of Geoacoustics, 1983. One article marked with a pencil asterisk: 'On the Storage and Replay of Vibrational Energy in Geological Strata.' The abstract notes that certain chalk-clay formations may retain and retransmit sound. The article ends with a note: RETRACTED. No reason given.

interactableDetails.description: An old map. Bermondsey, pre-1800.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: A survey map of Bermondsey, pre-1800. Seven buildings marked with a symbol you've seen somewhere else. The symbol is a small circle with a cross through it — and it appears on the brass key. The seven marked buildings still exist. Most of them.

interactableDetails.description: The owner's chair. The dent of years.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The chair holds the shape of someone who has sat in it every day for decades. Not just a worn cushion — the whole chair has adapted. Armrests angled to one body. The posture of the shop made permanent.

interactableDetails.description: The back room. Light through the doorway.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Light through the back room door at this hour makes a precise rectangle on the floor. The angle shifts fifteen degrees from when you arrived. The room is a sundial. The books on the shelves move in and out of its path as hours pass.

interactableDetails.description: Dust suspended in the window light. The same dust, always.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The light comes in at this angle for twenty minutes a day. The dust hangs in it as if stopped. You have been in this bookshop three times now. The dust is always in the same place.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain outside. In here: paper and silence. The weather becomes irrelevant.
weatherEffects.clear: Dust in the light from the high window. Every particle moving.

G07.bodySensation: The warmth of old paper. Shelves close on both sides. The floor uneven.
G07.permanentPresence: The retracted journal. The pencil asterisk.
G07.mythologicalImpression: The bookshop keeps things that were meant to be forgotten. That's what bookshops are for.

G08.name: The Park Path — Up the Hill
G08.body: Grass and mud depending on the week. The city drops behind you. Wind at the crest. Crows arguing.

from_G07: Up the hill. The bookshop below.
The park. The city drops behind.
Grass and crows. The observatory ahead.
from_G02: Down from the observatory. The city returns.
The hill in reverse. Heavier going down.
The park path back. Crows watching.

interactableDetails.description: A chestnut tree. Much older than the others.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: This chestnut has a girth that marks it as two hundred years old at minimum. It was here when the park was redesigned. When the observatory was expanded. When the tunnel was dug. It watched all of it. The bark has the texture of something that has been patient for a very long time.

interactableDetails.description: The feeling here. Before the hill crests.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Just before the hill crests, the feeling changes. Not the view — the view hasn't opened yet. But something in the air becomes expectant. As if the park is holding a breath it's been holding for centuries. Waiting for someone to arrive who hasn't arrived yet. Or has been arriving repeatedly.

interactableDetails.description: Two crows on the path. Not moving.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Two crows on the path, facing each other. Not fighting, not eating. Exchanging something in calls you can almost pattern. A structural conversation. Each call answered precisely. You slow. They don't move. The exchange continues.

interactableDetails.description: London from the hill. Different from the observatory.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: From here, London is at eye level, not below. The glass towers of Canary Wharf are companions, not objects. The scale shifts. This is the height at which the city stops being a subject and becomes a context.

interactableDetails.description: The path worn into the hill. Centuries of the same walk.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The grass grows up to the path and stops. The path is four hundred years old at minimum — you know this without knowing how you know it. The feet that made this path are gone. The path remains.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain on the hill. The path turns to mud at the edges. The grass smells of it.
weatherEffects.clear: The hill in clear air. The dome of the observatory visible. London below.

G08.bodySensation: The hill works your legs. The city drops. Wind finds you at the crest.
G08.permanentPresence: The crows. Arguing. On the path above.
G08.mythologicalImpression: People have climbed this hill to see the city for two thousand years. The hill has been watching them do it.

G09.name: The Thames Foreshore
G09.body: Wet clay and river silt. Broken pottery older than anything above the waterline. The water smells like iron. Nothing permanent underfoot.

from_L08: Down to the river. Greenwich below.
The river. The tide was here first.
from_B05: The river curves. The mast visible now.
from_G10: From the pub to the river edge.
Clay and iron and old water.
from_G04: The tunnel exit. River air immediately.
Out of the tunnel. The Thames.
From under the river to beside it.

interactableDetails.description: A pottery shard in the clay.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: You pry the shard from the clay. Slip-glazed earthenware, cream body. Medieval, maybe earlier. The river has held it since before the buildings above the bank existed. You hold something that last had a purpose seven hundred years ago. You put it back where it belongs.

interactableDetails.description: The river smell. Iron and time.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The Thames at the foreshore smells of iron — not rust, not industry. The iron of old water, tidal water, water that has been moving over the same clay for ten thousand years. You inhale. The smell is the oldest thing you've experienced in London.

interactableDetails.description: The river surface. The reflection is wrong.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The north bank in the reflection is sharper than the actual bank. More detail, cleaner edges. As if the river's version is better resolved than the real thing. You photograph it. Later, the print will show a building on the north bank that isn't there.

interactableDetails.description: The clay underfoot. Warmer than the air.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The foreshore clay is warmer than the air temperature. Not from sun — the sky is overcast. The warmth comes from the clay itself. Two metres down, the layer that hasn't moved in ten thousand years. It's warm. It's always been warm.

interactableDetails.description: The Thames mud. Dark grey. It smells of four thousand years.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: You sink half an inch before it holds you. This mud is the accumulated silt of every flood, every storm, every season London has ever had. You are standing on time. It is soft underfoot.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain on the river. The surface becomes a field of small explosions. Your footsteps sink deeper.
weatherEffects.clear: Low tide in clear light. The foreshore extends. Objects emerge.

G09.bodySensation: The clay yields underfoot. Nothing here is permanent. The river makes sure of it.
G09.permanentPresence: The pottery in the clay. There's always more.
G09.mythologicalImpression: The Thames foreshore is London's unconscious. What the city can't hold onto ends up here, in the clay.

G10.name: The Trafalgar Tavern
G10.body: Dark wood and hops. The bar is scarred. Windows uncleaned in a way that filters river light to gold.

from_G09: From the clay to the warmth.
The pub. Dry ground. A drink.
River light through the old glass.
from_G06: From white stone to dark wood.
The river through the windows. Gold.

interactableDetails.description: The bar top. Carved and burned and worn.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The bar top is a record. Burns from cigarettes, ring marks from glasses, initials carved deeply over shallower initials over deeper ones. A century of people declaring themselves in wood. The bar will outlast all of them.

interactableDetails.description: The windows. River light through old glass.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The windows haven't been cleaned in a way that matters. The accumulated grime filters the river light to amber. The pub has been making this specific light for decades. If you cleaned the windows, you'd ruin it.

interactableDetails.description: The corner table. The same faces.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The corner table has the same configuration of people every evening. Not the same people — different bodies, different decades, the same dynamic. A person who talks too much, one who listens well, one who observes. The pub casts the same three roles every night.

interactableDetails.description: The floor. Something humming below.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Your foot on the floor: a vibration from below. Too low to be pipes. Too steady to be traffic. The cellar of the Trafalgar goes deeper than cellar plans show. Something in it produces a frequency that the pub floor transmits to your feet every evening.

interactableDetails.description: The Thames through the pub windows. Amber light through grime.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The windows face the river. They have not been cleaned in a long time — not neglect, design. The grime filters the light to amber. The Thames through this glass looks the same as it did two hundred years ago.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain on the windows. The gold light dims slightly. The pub compensates with warmth.
weatherEffects.clear: The river catches sunset and throws it through the windows. The pub turns orange.

G10.bodySensation: The warmth hits immediately. Dark wood and river light. Your shoulders drop.
G10.permanentPresence: The river light through the uncleaned glass.
G10.mythologicalImpression: The Trafalgar has been watching the river longer than the city has been watching the Trafalgar.

B01.name: The Antiques Market
B01.body: Dawn cold and flask coffee. Tables of objects with no common logic. The regulars know each other by nod. Everything here had a previous life.

from_B09: From the corner into the market. The objects watch.
Dawn. The serious buyers are already here.
Tables of things. Each one from somewhere.
from_B02: From white walls to cluttered tables.
The gallery to the market. Both curate.

interactableDetails.description: A brass key on a cloth. Wrong metal for its age.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The key is brass but the weight is wrong for the size. No manufacturer's mark from any foundry you know. The vendor watches your hand reach for it but says nothing. Something in the metal is warm.

interactableDetails.description: A clock face with no hands.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: A clock face, brass, no hands. The roman numerals are correct but the XII is where the VI should be. Not broken — made this way. A clock that reads backwards.

interactableDetails.description: A framed photograph. The street behind it.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: A photograph of this street, from this approximate angle, dated 1923. The market is there. The same arrangement of tables. The same space between them. The people are different. The objects are the same.

interactableDetails.description: The square. Surprisingly warm for the hour.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The market square retains heat at dawn even when the surrounding streets are cold. Not from the traders' lamps. From below. The ground here has been generating warmth since before anyone was keeping records of it.

interactableDetails.description: The vendor's flask. He shares it without asking.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: He pours without looking at you. The flask is passed to the next stall holder. Passed back. A circuit. Everyone in the market part of the same conversation, not a word exchanged. The flask is the sentence.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain on the plastic sheeting over the tables. The vendors ignore it.
weatherEffects.clear: Dawn light on brass and glass. Everything flash for a moment.

B01.bodySensation: Cold hands. The flask of tea passed without asking.
B01.permanentPresence: The key on the cloth. Always the same position.
B01.mythologicalImpression: The market has been here longer than the street. The street was built for the market, not the other way.

B02.name: The Gallery
B02.body: White walls, concrete floor, temperature-controlled air that smells of nothing. Your footsteps are the loudest thing. The receptionist's typing second loudest.

from_B01: From the objects to the absence of objects.
White walls. The opposite of the market.
The gallery. Your footsteps too loud.
from_B03: From the arch noise to this silence.
Concrete floor, both places. Different purposes.
The gallery. The quietest room in Bermondsey.
from_B06: The co-working space to the gallery. Same street.
White walls. You adjust your eyes.

interactableDetails.description: The painting on the back wall. Something about it.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The painting on the back wall draws you. You stand in front of it. The subject is London — Bermondsey Street from above, at dusk, the shadows at odd angles. The date: 2035. The paint is old. The scene is nine years from now.

interactableDetails.description: The painting. You come back and it's changed.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: You stand in front of the painting. It's moved. Not rehung — the paint has changed. A building that was in the background is now in the foreground. A figure that wasn't there before stands in the doorway. The figure has your posture.

interactableDetails.description: The room. The way sound moves in it.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: You hum a single note. The gallery captures it and returns it transformed — not echo, not reverb. An answer. A different note that harmonises with yours. The concrete floor and white walls aren't reflecting the sound. They're participating in it.

interactableDetails.description: The concrete floor. Older than the gallery.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The concrete floor has been polished for decades but you can see the original surface around the edges: hand-poured, aggregate showing. The gallery repurposed an industrial floor from a building that was here before. The floor has carried weight it doesn't speak about.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain outside. In here: nothing. The gallery has decided not to participate in weather.
weatherEffects.clear: Light through the high windows makes columns on the floor. They move as hours pass.

B02.bodySensation: Your footsteps are too loud. The air has been conditioned into perfect neutrality.
B02.permanentPresence: The painting on the back wall. Facing you.
B02.mythologicalImpression: The gallery shows what the city chooses to remember. Tonight the city remembers something that hasn't happened yet.

B03.name: The Railway Arch Workshop
B03.body: Brick curves overhead. The echo makes every sound twice. Tools hanging in deliberate order. Sawdust. Metal shavings. The smell of work.

from_B02: From the gallery to the workshop. Both serious.
White to brick. One kind of attention to another.
from_B04: Out of the dark into the noise.
The warehouse at night. The arch in daylight.
From one industrial space to another. Different clocks.
from_B08: Down from the roof. Into the arch.
The rooftop to the workshop. Same structure.

interactableDetails.description: The arch overhead. The echo it produces.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: You drop a tool. It clatters. Then clatters again, a second after. Then a third echo, below the natural reverberation of the arch. A third echo in a space that only has two reflecting surfaces. You drop it again. Three echoes. Every time.

interactableDetails.description: The tools on the wall. A system.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The tools are hung in an order that isn't alphabetical, chronological, or by size. After a moment you see it: by age. The oldest tools on the left, newest on the right. An archive. The oldest tool is a handmade implement — pre-industrial, possibly pre-Georgian.

interactableDetails.description: The arch bricks. Look closely.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The arch bricks aren't uniform. Some carry a maker's stamp: 1843. Others have no stamp — older, handmade. The railway arch was built incorporating a structure that predated it. Something was already here, and the Victorian engineers built the arch over it instead of through it.

interactableDetails.description: The floor. Sawdust and metal. Warm.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The floor retains warmth through the night. Not from the tools — they're cold. The warmth comes up from below, through the concrete, from whatever the arch was built over. The workshop has always been warmer than the street outside.

interactableDetails.description: The curve of the arch overhead. Victorian brick, Victorian weight.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The arch predates everything in the room by a hundred and fifty years. Everything else — the tools, the workbench, the lights — is provisional. The arch is permanent. It will be here when everything else is gone.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain on the arch roof. Amplified, dulled, then amplified again. Three layers of rain.
weatherEffects.clear: Morning light through the arch entrance. A spotlight on the workshop floor.

B03.bodySensation: The echo returns your footsteps one beat behind. Sawdust underfoot. The smell of iron.
B03.permanentPresence: The tool wall. Everything in order. New on the right. Old on the left.
B03.mythologicalImpression: The arch was built over something older. The thing underneath doesn't know it has a railway arch on top of it.

B04.name: The Warehouse — Night
B04.body: Concrete and dark and the sound of your own breathing. The guard's torch makes everything a stage set. Temperature drops further in.

from_B03: The arch workshop to the warehouse. Night coming.
From tools to darkness. Same industrial spine.
The warehouse entrance. The guard's torch.
from_B05: From the river to the concrete.
The Thames path behind. The warehouse ahead.
Dark inside. The guard's torch moving.

interactableDetails.description: A survey marker in the floor. Official-looking.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: A metal disc set into the concrete. Official markings but no agency name you recognise. The depth reading is wrong: 400 metres. Standard surveys reach 30. Scratched in the concrete beside it, in careful letters: 'Still sleeping. Check annually.'

interactableDetails.description: The shelving. Different from last time.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: You were here before. The shelving was on the left. Now it's on the right. You check the door: same door. You walk the perimeter: same dimensions. The shelving moved. The warehouse rearranged itself.

interactableDetails.description: A cold pocket in the dark.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Further in, the temperature drops suddenly and locally. Not air conditioning — there's no ventilation. A pocket of cold that has no mechanical source. You stand in it. The cold has a shape. It occupies the space where something used to be stored that isn't here anymore.

interactableDetails.description: The floor. A vibration from below.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Your feet on the concrete: a vibration below the threshold of sound. You crouch. Press your palm flat. It's rhythmic. Not pipes, not traffic. A pulse. The warehouse floor breathes once every four seconds. You count it for two minutes. Never misses.

interactableDetails.description: The warehouse entrance at night. The darkness is not empty.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The darkness inside is different from the darkness outside. Outside: absence of light. Inside: something that replaced the light with something else. You stand at the threshold for a moment longer than you meant to.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain on the warehouse roof. Distant, muffled. Inside: absolute still.
weatherEffects.clear: Moonlight through the high vents. The warehouse gets one stripe of light.

B04.bodySensation: Cold increases with distance from the door. Your breathing is the loudest sound.
B04.permanentPresence: The survey marker in the floor. Still sleeping.
B04.mythologicalImpression: The warehouse stores what isn't there anymore. That's more than you can carry.

B05.name: The Thames Path — Rotherhithe
