B05.body: The river wide here, slow. Industrial sounds opposite. A bench bolted to the railing faces the water. The bench is cold.

from_B04: Out of the warehouse. The river.
Dark to open. The Thames wider here.
Cold warehouse to cold river. Different cold.
from_B07: The church behind me. The river ahead.
The path opens. The sky opens.
from_G09: The clay behind me. Different bank now.
The longest walk. More sky. Deptford next.

interactableDetails.description: The railing. Something scratched into it.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Scratched into the railing, very faint, at eye height if you lean on it: 'It noticed me in 2017. I noticed it back. That was my mistake. The noticing doesn't stop. Walk away if you can.' The metal around the letters is darker — polished by someone running their fingers over it. Many times.

interactableDetails.description: The bench bolted to the railing. Always cold.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The bench is cold even in summer. You sit. The cold comes through your clothes immediately. Not unpleasant — focused. The cold holds your attention on the river. Maybe that's why the bench was bolted here.

interactableDetails.description: The river. Unusually slow here.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The Thames at Rotherhithe is wider and slower than upriver. The surface barely moves. You take a photograph of the reflection. The south bank in the image has buildings that aren't there — not yet, perhaps. The river shows the next draft.

interactableDetails.description: Industrial sound from the opposite bank.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The sound from the north bank — cranes, machinery, something unidentifiable — arrives across the water transformed. The river translates it into something almost melodic. Each industrial sound arrives as its harmonic. The Thames is a tuning fork.

interactableDetails.description: The Thames here. It never quite holds still.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The river at Rotherhithe is wider and slower than at Limehouse. The current barely moves. You can watch the same piece of water for thirty seconds before it is gone. Plenty of time to see what it carries.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain on the Thames. The surface becomes chaos. The industrial sounds stop. Then return.
weatherEffects.clear: The river in clear air. You can see further than makes sense.

B05.bodySensation: The bench cold through clothing. River wind from the east.
B05.permanentPresence: The message on the railing. The bench that faces the water.
B05.mythologicalImpression: The Thames here is at its most patient. It has been watching this bank change for two thousand years.

B06.name: The Co-Working Space
B06.body: Laptop fans and coffee and the silence of people concentrating near each other. Exposed brick. A plant nobody claims. Same Wi-Fi password two years.

from_B09: From the corner. Into the working quiet.
The street corner to the co-working space.
from_B02: From the gallery to the work.
Both on Bermondsey Street. Different intentions.
The co-working space. More laptop fans.
from_B10: From the vinyl to the wifi.
Music to work. The same street.

interactableDetails.description: A plant in the corner. Nobody claims it.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The plant is watered but nobody will say by whom. You ask three people. None claims it. The plant has been in this corner for two years, tended by consensus, owned by none. A commons. A small version of what every street eventually becomes.

interactableDetails.description: The Wi-Fi password on the exposed brick.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The password is written in marker on the brick. Unchanged for two years — everyone who comes knows it. In London a two-year Wi-Fi password is a permanent fixture. You write it in your notebook without knowing why.

interactableDetails.description: The people here. The web between them.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: You watch the room. The five people here don't know each other, are not talking. But connections run between them: the woman knows the man's client, the man worked with the person in the corner before she was a freelancer. Two degrees. In this room, everyone is two steps from everyone. The network made visible.

interactableDetails.description: The exposed brick. Older than the street.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The exposed brick isn't Victorian. You know Victorian brickwork. This is older — irregular, hand-fired. The building was here before the street was built around it. The co-working space, laptop fans and all, occupies a structure that predates most of London's infrastructure.

interactableDetails.description: A notebook left on a hot desk. No name. Half full.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: No name on the cover. Opened: meeting notes, lists, a phone number with no name, and halfway down page twelve, a sentence that stops mid-word. The handwriting changes before it stops. As if someone else finished it.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain outside. The co-working space doesn't notice. It is its own weather.
weatherEffects.clear: Light through the street window. People look up from screens briefly.

B06.bodySensation: Warmth from the laptop fans. Coffee steam. The hum of productivity.
B06.permanentPresence: The plant. The Wi-Fi password. The connections nobody speaks aloud.
B06.mythologicalImpression: The network visible in this room is the whole city compressed. In every room in London, everyone is two steps from everyone.

B07.name: The Old Church — St Mary Magdalen
B07.body: Stone standing since 1290. Inside: kneelers worn to the shape of specific knees. Afternoon light the morning wouldn't recognise.

from_B09: The corner to the church. A minute's walk.
Oldest building here. You can feel it.
from_B05: The Thames path to the church. The oldest things in Bermondsey.
From water to what water built.

interactableDetails.description: A door in the floor. The crypt.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: An iron ring set into the stone floor. A crypt door, old as the church. The iron is worn smooth from centuries of hands. The ring is cold even in summer. The door is locked. The keyhole is a shape you've seen somewhere recently.

interactableDetails.description: The kneelers. Worn to specific shapes.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The kneelers are worn down unevenly. Not from use — from specific use. The same knees, the same bodies, for generations. Each kneeler holds the shape of a person who is not here. You kneel in one of them. It fits you wrong, which means you belong to a different story.

interactableDetails.description: The afternoon light. It changes the space.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: At this hour, the light through the south window turns the stone from grey to gold. The same stone that looks ancient and cold in the morning looks alive in the afternoon. Hawksmoor understood this: all his churches were built to do things with light. This church predates Hawksmoor by four hundred years. It understood it first.

interactableDetails.description: A stone at the base of the wall. Different.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: One block at the base of the wall is older than the church. Pre-Norman — possibly Roman. The church was built using this stone as a foundation marker. A symbol carved into it. The same symbol as the key. One of the seven.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain on the churchyard. Inside: dry, cold, permanent.
weatherEffects.clear: Afternoon light through the south window. The stone goes gold.

B07.bodySensation: The cold of old stone. Your knees in the worn kneeler. Someone else's shape.
B07.permanentPresence: The crypt door. The iron ring worn smooth.
B07.mythologicalImpression: The church was built on something older. The thing older was built on something older still. London's geology is also its theology.

B08.name: The Rooftop
B08.body: Wind and all of it — cranes, river, the city's breath. Bitumen and gravel underfoot. A folding chair, faded, still unfolded. Enormous sky.

from_B03: Up through the arch to the roof.
The workshop below. The whole city above.

interactableDetails.description: The streets below. From up here they form something.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: From this height, at this angle, the streets form letters. Not English. Not any alphabet you recognise. But unmistakably letters — the angles deliberate, the spacing proportional. The city arranged itself into a sentence nobody has read. The light is failing. You photograph what you can.

interactableDetails.description: A folding chair. Unfolded. Waiting.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The chair is faded but not abandoned. Someone comes here. The chair is positioned to face the river and the cranes. A specific view, chosen deliberately. The arms are worn where the same hands always rest. Whoever comes here comes to watch the same thing, for hours, often enough to wear the paint.

interactableDetails.description: The wind up here. Different from street level.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The wind at rooftop level is unbroken. At street level London interrupts it constantly. Up here it arrives whole, from wherever it started. You face it. Your body reads the direction — east, off the river, carrying the smell of salt that gets this far inland only at certain tides. The city has a wind signature.

interactableDetails.description: The cranes. Their movement in the wind.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Four cranes visible. Each swaying at a different rate in the wind. You watch their movement for long enough to find the pattern: they're not random. Their periods form a ratio. 3:4:5:6. A chord in rotation. The cranes are playing Bermondsey.

interactableDetails.description: The city from above. Every crane. Every tower. London building itself.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: From up here you can count nine cranes. London is always mid-construction, always becoming something it is not yet. You wonder if the city knows what it is building toward, or if it is doing what it has always done — becoming, without plan.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain up here with nothing between you and the sky. You are the highest point for some distance.
weatherEffects.clear: Clear evening light. The city spread out. Everything visible. For once.

B08.bodySensation: Wind unbroken. Gravel underfoot. Sky above, enormous.
B08.permanentPresence: The folding chair. Unfolded. Facing the river.
B08.mythologicalImpression: From up here the city reveals its structure. What looked random from street level was always legible from above.

B09.name: The Street Corner — Long Lane
B09.body: Traffic and a sandwich shop and pigeons competing with sparrows. Unremarkable. That's the point. What appears here shouldn't.

from_L10: Different. More brick in it. More weight.
Bermondsey Street. Old side. New side.
from_B01: From the market to the corner.
Long Lane. The city at its most plain.
from_B06: From the working quiet to the street.
The corner. Unremarkable is a choice.
from_B07: The church behind. The street again.
The corner. Something always almost happens.

interactableDetails.description: The corner itself. What keeps drawing your attention.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: You've passed this corner a dozen times. Each time you look, something appears to be changing — then isn't. The sandwich shop has been there thirty years. The corner has been a corner since the streets were laid. But there's a quality to it. An expectation. The corner is waiting for something.

interactableDetails.description: A gap in the street. Three addresses, no buildings.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Three consecutive addresses on Bermondsey Street with no buildings. Not empty lots — the buildings on either side touch. But three numbers in the sequence exist: mail is delivered there. The letterbox is in the wall of the adjacent building. Someone receives mail at an address that has no physical structure.

interactableDetails.description: A drawing on the pavement. Fresh chalk.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: A chalk drawing on the pavement. A person at the center with lines radiating to other figures. A web, a connection map. Child's hand. The proportions are wrong in a way that's not childlike — they're correct for something more complex than a child should draw. Each figure at the end of a line has a name. You recognise three of them.

interactableDetails.description: The pigeons. Something about their pattern.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The pigeons at this corner avoid a specific patch of pavement. Not food, not a cat — they simply land around it and walk around it, every time. You stand on the avoided spot. The air is different. Slightly pressurised. As if something is pushing up from below.

interactableDetails.description: The corner pub sign. The name is new. The bracket is not.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The mounting bracket is Victorian iron — you can see where the old bolts left marks. The current sign is modern, painted, cheerful. The ghost of the previous name is faintly legible beneath the new paint. Something was here before.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain on the corner. The pigeons shelter under the awning. You don't.
weatherEffects.clear: The corner in flat afternoon light. Nothing to hide behind.

B09.bodySensation: Traffic from two directions. Pigeons underfoot. The smell of yesterday's sandwich.
B09.permanentPresence: The avoided patch of pavement. The pigeons always know.
B09.mythologicalImpression: The most ordinary place in the game. What appears here shouldn't. That's the design.

B10.name: The Vinyl Shop
B10.body: Dust on plastic sleeves. A turntable playing something pressed when this street was different. Crates filed in a system only the owner understands.

from_B06: From the co-working to the records.
Both on Bermondsey Street. Different kinds of stored work.
The vinyl shop. The turntable already playing.

interactableDetails.description: The turntable. Playing something you half-recognise.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The record playing is from the mid-1960s. A session recording, not a famous release. After a moment you place the melody — it's the one the barista hums. The same tune, professionally arranged, pressed to vinyl forty years before the barista was born. The melody predates the person carrying it.

interactableDetails.description: The crates. A filing system.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The crates aren't filed alphabetically or by genre. After a moment you see the system: by connection. Records filed near records that share a musician, a producer, a session, a building. The whole shop is a network diagram, filed in vinyl. Every record touching at least one other.

interactableDetails.description: A sleeve at the back. Much older than the others.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: A sleeve at the back of the deepest crate. Not vinyl — shellac, 78 RPM, pre-war. The label is from a company that doesn't exist. The address on the label: Bermondsey Street. This record was made on this street when the street was something other than what it is now. You play it. It plays something back.

interactableDetails.description: Dust in the light. The shop has its own atmosphere.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: The shop light is at the right angle to make the dust visible. Every particle moving in its own pattern, independent of the others. The shop's air is full of material — sleeve dust, vinyl particles, paper fibres. Every record that's been handled here is still here, in the air.

interactableDetails.description: The smell of the shop. Old paper and vinyl and something underneath.
interactableDetails.discovery_text: Old paper. Dry vinyl. A faint basement smell under that — cold brick, stale air, something that has been sealed a long time. Not unpleasant. The smell of things kept. The smell of things that outlasted the people who left them.

weatherEffects.rain: Rain outside. The turntable plays. The shop does not notice weather.
weatherEffects.clear: Afternoon light across the crates. The dust dances.

B10.bodySensation: Dust in the air. The crackle of the turntable. Sleeve edges under your fingers.
B10.mythologicalImpression: Every record in here is a previous version of this street, compressed and stored. The shop is an archive of before.
